or **late medieval period** was the [period](/wiki/Periodization "Periodization") of [European history](/wiki/History_of_Europe "History of Europe") lasting from 1300 to 1500 AD. The late Middle Ages followed the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages") and preceded the onset of the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period") (and in much of Europe, the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance")).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Period of European history between AD 1300 and 1500

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Europe_in_the_14th_Century.jpg/330px-Europe_in_the_14th_Century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europe_in_the_14th_Century.jpg)

Europe and the Mediterranean region, c. 1354

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Great_famine.jpg/330px-Great_famine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Great_famine.jpg)

From the Apocalypse in a *[Biblia Pauperum](/wiki/Biblia_Pauperum "Biblia Pauperum")* illuminated at [Erfurt](/wiki/Erfurt "Erfurt") around the time of the [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_1315%E2%80%931317 "Great Famine of 1315–1317"). Death sits astride a lion whose long tail ends in a ball of flame (Hell). Famine points to her hungry mouth.

The **Late Middle Ages** or **late medieval period** was the [period](/wiki/Periodization "Periodization") of [European history](/wiki/History_of_Europe "History of Europe") lasting from 1300 to 1500 AD. The late Middle Ages followed the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages") and preceded the onset of the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period") (and in much of Europe, the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance")).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Around 1350, centuries of prosperity and growth in Europe came to a halt. A series of [famines](/wiki/Famine "Famine") and [plagues](/wiki/Plague_(disease) "Plague (disease)"), including the [Great Famine of 1315–1317](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_1315%E2%80%931317 "Great Famine of 1315–1317") and the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"), reduced the population to around half of what it had been before the calamities.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Along with depopulation came social unrest and [endemic warfare](/wiki/Endemic_warfare "Endemic warfare"). [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") and [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") experienced serious peasant uprisings, such as the [Jacquerie](/wiki/Jacquerie "Jacquerie") and the [Peasants' Revolt](/wiki/Peasants%27_Revolt "Peasants' Revolt"), as well as over a century of intermittent conflict, the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"). To add to the many problems of the period, the unity of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") was temporarily shattered by the [Western Schism](/wiki/Western_Schism "Western Schism"). Collectively, those events are sometimes called the [crisis of the late Middle Ages](/wiki/Crisis_of_the_late_Middle_Ages "Crisis of the late Middle Ages").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Despite the crises, the 14th century was also a time of great progress in the arts and sciences. Following a renewed interest in ancient [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") texts that took root in the High Middle Ages, the [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") began. The absorption of Latin texts had started before the [Renaissance of the 12th century](/wiki/Renaissance_of_the_12th_century "Renaissance of the 12th century") through contact with Arabs during the [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades"), but the availability of important Greek texts accelerated with the [fall of Constantinople](/wiki/Fall_of_Constantinople "Fall of Constantinople") to the [Ottoman Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), when many [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") scholars had to seek refuge in the West, particularly Italy.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Combined with this influx of classical ideas was the invention of printing, which facilitated the dissemination of the printed word and democratized learning. These two developments would later contribute to the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"). Toward the end of the period, the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") began. The expansion of the Ottoman Empire cut off trading possibilities with the East. Europeans were forced to seek new trading routes, leading to the Spanish expedition under [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") to the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") in 1492 and [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama "Vasco da Gama")'s voyage to Africa and India in 1498. Their discoveries strengthened the economy and power of European nations.

The changes brought about by these developments have led many scholars to view this period as the end of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and the beginning of [modern history](/wiki/Modern_history "Modern history") and of [early modern Europe](/wiki/Early_modern_Europe "Early modern Europe"). However, the division is somewhat artificial, since ancient learning was never entirely absent from European society.[[5]](#cite_note-5) As a result, there was [developmental continuity](/wiki/Continuity_thesis "Continuity thesis") between the [ancient age](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history") (via [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity")) and the [modern age](/wiki/Modern_age "Modern age").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Some historians, particularly in Italy, prefer not to speak of the late Middle Ages at all; rather, they see the high period of the Middle Ages transitioning to the Renaissance and the modern era.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The term "late Middle Ages" refers to one of the three periods of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), along with the early Middle Ages and the High Middle Ages. [Leonardo Bruni](/wiki/Leonardo_Bruni "Leonardo Bruni") was the first historian to use the tripartite periodization in his *History of the Florentine People* (1442).[[8]](#cite_note-Hankins-8) [Flavio Biondo](/wiki/Flavio_Biondo "Flavio Biondo") used a similar framework in *Decades of History from the Deterioration of the Roman Empire* (1439–1453). The tripartite periodization became standard after the German historian [Christoph Cellarius](/wiki/Christoph_Cellarius "Christoph Cellarius") published *Universal History Divided into an Ancient, Medieval, and New Period* (1683).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For 18th-century historians studying the 14th and 15th centuries, the central theme was the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), with its rediscovery of ancient learning and the emergence of an individual spirit.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The heart of this rediscovery lies in Italy, where, in the words of [Jacob Burckhardt](/wiki/Jacob_Burckhardt "Jacob Burckhardt"), "Man became a spiritual individual and recognized himself as such."[[10]](#cite_note-10) This proposition was later challenged, and it was argued that the 12th century was a period of greater cultural achievement.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

As economic and demographic methods were applied to the study of history, the trend was increasingly to see the late Middle Ages as a period of recession and crisis. [Belgian](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") historian [Henri Pirenne](/wiki/Henri_Pirenne "Henri Pirenne") continued the subdivision of [Early](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), [High](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages"), and late Middle Ages in the years around [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[[12]](#cite_note-12) Yet it was his [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") colleague, [Johan Huizinga](/wiki/Johan_Huizinga "Johan Huizinga"), who was primarily responsible for popularising the pessimistic view of the late Middle Ages, with his book *[The Autumn of the Middle Ages](/wiki/The_Autumn_of_the_Middle_Ages "The Autumn of the Middle Ages")* (1919).[[13]](#cite_note-13) To Huizinga, whose research focused on France and the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries") rather than Italy, despair and decline were the main themes, not rebirth.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Modern historiography on the period has reached a consensus between the two extremes of innovation and crisis. It is now generally acknowledged that conditions were vastly different north and south of the Alps, and the term "late Middle Ages" is often avoided entirely within Italian historiography.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The term "Renaissance" is still considered useful for describing certain intellectual, cultural, or artistic developments but not as the defining feature of an entire European historical epoch.[[17]](#cite_note-Bradyxvii-17) The period from the early 14th century up until – and sometimes including – the 16th century is rather seen as characterized by other trends: demographic and economic decline followed by recovery, the end of Western religious unity and the subsequent emergence of the [nation-state](/wiki/Nation-state "Nation-state"), and the expansion of European influence onto the rest of the world.[[17]](#cite_note-Bradyxvii-17)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The limits of [Christian Europe](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom") were still being defined in the 14th and 15th centuries. While the [Grand Duchy of Moscow](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Moscow "Grand Duchy of Moscow") was beginning to repel the [Mongols](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire"), and the [Iberian](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") kingdoms were completing the [Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista") of the peninsula and turning their attention outward, the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") fell under the dominance of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire").[[a]](#cite_note-For_references,_see_below-18) Meanwhile, the remaining nations of the continent were locked in almost constant international or internal conflict.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

The situation gradually led to the consolidation of central authority and the emergence of the [nation state](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state").[[19]](#cite_note-BradyJones-20) The financial demands of war necessitated higher levels of taxation, resulting in the emergence of representative bodies – most notably the [English Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England").[[20]](#cite_note-21) The growth of secular authority was further aided by the decline of the papacy with the [Western Schism](/wiki/Western_Schism "Western Schism") and the coming of the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

### Northern Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Northern Europe")]

Main articles: [Denmark in the Middle Ages](/wiki/History_of_Denmark#Middle_Ages "History of Denmark"), [Norway in the Middle Ages](/wiki/History_of_Norway#Middle_Ages "History of Norway"), [Sweden in the Middle Ages](/wiki/History_of_Sweden_(800%E2%80%931521)#Middle_Ages "History of Sweden (800–1521)"), and [Kalmar Union](/wiki/Kalmar_Union "Kalmar Union")

After the failed union of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") and [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") of 1319–1365, the pan-Scandinavian [Kalmar Union](/wiki/Kalmar_Union "Kalmar Union"), that also included [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), was instituted in 1397.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The Swedes were reluctant members of the union from the start. In an attempt to subdue the Swedes, King [Christian II of Denmark](/wiki/Christian_II_of_Denmark "Christian II of Denmark") had large numbers of the Swedish aristocracy killed in the [Stockholm Bloodbath](/wiki/Stockholm_Bloodbath "Stockholm Bloodbath") of 1520. Yet this measure only led to further hostilities, and Sweden broke away for good in 1523.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Norway, on the other hand, became an inferior party of the union and remained united with Denmark until 1814.

[Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") benefited from its relative isolation and was the last [Scandinavian](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") country to be struck by the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death").[[24]](#cite_note-25) Meanwhile, the [Norse colony in Greenland](/wiki/Norse_Greenland "Norse Greenland") died out, probably under extreme weather conditions in the 15th century.[[25]](#cite_note-26) These conditions might have been the effect of the [Little Ice Age](/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age").[[26]](#cite_note-27)

### Northwest Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Northwest Europe")]

Main articles: [England in the Late Middle Ages](/wiki/England_in_the_Late_Middle_Ages "England in the Late Middle Ages"), [Scotland in the Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Late_Middle_Ages "Scotland in the Late Middle Ages"), [Wales in the Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Wales_in_the_Late_Middle_Ages "Wales in the Late Middle Ages"), and [History of Ireland (1169–1536)](/wiki/History_of_Ireland_(1169%E2%80%931536) "History of Ireland (1169–1536)")

Further information: [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"), [Wars of the Roses](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Roses "Wars of the Roses"), and [Wars of Scottish Independence](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Schlacht_von_Azincourt.jpg/250px-Schlacht_von_Azincourt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schlacht_von_Azincourt.jpg)

The Battle of Agincourt, 15th-century miniature, [Enguerrand de Monstrelet](/wiki/Enguerrand_de_Monstrelet "Enguerrand de Monstrelet")

The death of [Alexander III of Scotland](/wiki/Alexander_III_of_Scotland "Alexander III of Scotland") in 1286 threw the country into a [succession crisis](/wiki/Succession_crisis "Succession crisis"), and the English king, [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England"), was brought in to arbitrate. Edward claimed overlordship over Scotland, leading to the [Wars of Scottish Independence](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence").[[27]](#cite_note-28) The English were eventually defeated, and the Scots were able to develop a stronger state under the [Stewarts](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart").[[28]](#cite_note-29)

From 1337, England's attention was largely directed towards France in the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War").[[29]](#cite_note-30) [Henry V's](/wiki/Henry_V_of_England "Henry V of England") victory at the [Battle of Agincourt](/wiki/Battle_of_Agincourt "Battle of Agincourt") in 1415 briefly paved the way for a unification of the two kingdoms, but his son [Henry VI](/wiki/Henry_VI_of_England "Henry VI of England") soon squandered all previous gains.[[30]](#cite_note-31) The loss of France led to discontent at home. Soon after the end of the war in 1453, the dynastic struggles of the [Wars of the Roses](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Roses "Wars of the Roses") (c. 1455–1485) began, involving the rival dynasties of the [House of Lancaster](/wiki/House_of_Lancaster "House of Lancaster") and [House of York](/wiki/House_of_York "House of York").[[31]](#cite_note-32)

The war ended in the accession of [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") of the [House of Tudor](/wiki/House_of_Tudor "House of Tudor"), who continued the work started by the Yorkist kings of building a strong, centralized monarchy.[[32]](#cite_note-33) While England's attention was thus directed elsewhere, the [Hiberno-Norman](/wiki/Hiberno-Norman "Hiberno-Norman") lords in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") were becoming gradually more assimilated into Irish society, and the island was allowed to develop virtual independence under English overlordship.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

### Western Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Western Europe")]

Main articles: [France in the Middle Ages](/wiki/France_in_the_Middle_Ages "France in the Middle Ages"), [Duchy of Burgundy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Burgundy "Duchy of Burgundy"), and [Burgundian Netherlands](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Map_France_1477-en.svg/250px-Map_France_1477-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_France_1477-en.svg)

France by 1477: a mosaic of feudal territories

The [French](/wiki/France "France") [House of Valois](/wiki/House_of_Valois "House of Valois"), which followed the [House of Capet](/wiki/House_of_Capet "House of Capet") in 1328, was at its outset marginalized in its own country, first by the English invading forces of the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War") and later by the powerful [Duchy of Burgundy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Burgundy "Duchy of Burgundy").[[34]](#cite_note-35) The emergence of [Joan of Arc](/wiki/Joan_of_Arc "Joan of Arc") as a military leader changed the course of war in favour of the French, and the initiative was carried further by King [Louis XI](/wiki/Louis_XI_of_France "Louis XI of France").[[35]](#cite_note-36)

Meanwhile, [Charles the Bold](/wiki/Charles_the_Bold "Charles the Bold"), [Duke of Burgundy](/wiki/Duke_of_Burgundy "Duke of Burgundy"), met resistance in his attempts to consolidate his possessions, particularly from the [Swiss Confederation](/wiki/Old_Swiss_Confederacy "Old Swiss Confederacy") formed in 1291.[[36]](#cite_note-37) When Charles was killed in the [Burgundian Wars](/wiki/Burgundian_Wars "Burgundian Wars") at the [Battle of Nancy](/wiki/Battle_of_Nancy "Battle of Nancy") in 1477, the [Duchy of Burgundy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Burgundy "Duchy of Burgundy") was reclaimed by France.[[37]](#cite_note-38) At the same time, the [County of Burgundy](/wiki/County_of_Burgundy "County of Burgundy") and the wealthy [Burgundian Netherlands](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands") came into the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") under [Habsburg](/wiki/Habsburg "Habsburg") control, setting up conflict for centuries to come.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

### Central Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Central Europe")]

Main articles: [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), [Lands of the Bohemian Crown (1348–1526)](/wiki/Lands_of_the_Bohemian_Crown_(1348%E2%80%931526) "Lands of the Bohemian Crown (1348–1526)"), [History of Poland during the Jagiellonian dynasty](/wiki/History_of_Poland_during_the_Jagiellonian_dynasty "History of Poland during the Jagiellonian dynasty"), [Old Swiss Confederacy](/wiki/Old_Swiss_Confederacy "Old Swiss Confederacy"), [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania"), and [Kingdom of Hungary (1301–1526)](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1301%E2%80%931526) "Kingdom of Hungary (1301–1526)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Silver_mine%2C_Kutna_Hora.jpg/250px-Silver_mine%2C_Kutna_Hora.jpg)](/wiki/File:Silver_mine,_Kutna_Hora.jpg)

Silver mining and processing in [Kutná Hora](/wiki/Kutn%C3%A1_Hora "Kutná Hora"), Bohemia, 15th century

[Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia") prospered in the 14th century, and the [Golden Bull of 1356](/wiki/Golden_Bull_of_1356 "Golden Bull of 1356") made the king of Bohemia the first among the [imperial electors](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector"), but the [Hussite revolution](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars") threw the country into crisis.[[39]](#cite_note-40) The [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") passed to the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") in 1438, where it remained until its [dissolution](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Holy_Roman_Empire "Dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire") in 1806.[[40]](#cite_note-41) Yet in spite of the [extensive territories held by the Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"), the Empire itself remained fragmented, and much real power and influence lay with the individual principalities.[[41]](#cite_note-42) In addition, financial institutions, such as the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") and the [Fugger](/wiki/Fugger "Fugger") family, held great power, on both economic and political levels.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

The [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1301%E2%80%931526) "Kingdom of Hungary (1301–1526)") experienced a golden age during the 14th century.[[43]](#cite_note-44) In particular the reigns of the [Angevin](/wiki/Capetian_House_of_Anjou "Capetian House of Anjou") kings [Charles Robert](/wiki/Charles_I_of_Hungary "Charles I of Hungary") (1308–42) and his son [Louis the Great](/wiki/Louis_the_Great "Louis the Great") (1342–82) were marked by success.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The country grew wealthy as the main European supplier of gold and silver.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Louis the Great led successful campaigns from Lithuania to Southern Italy, and from Poland to Northern Greece.

He had the greatest military potential of the 14th century with his enormous armies (often over 100,000 men). Meanwhile, [Poland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland_(1025%E2%80%931385) "Kingdom of Poland (1025–1385)")'s attention was turned eastwards, as its [Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") with [Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania") created an enormous entity in the region.[[46]](#cite_note-47) The union, and the conversion of Lithuania, also marked the end of [paganism](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism") in Europe.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Beckov_Horne_nadvorie_01.jpg/250px-Beckov_Horne_nadvorie_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beckov_Horne_nadvorie_01.jpg)

[Ruins](/wiki/Ruins "Ruins") of [Beckov Castle](/wiki/Beckov_Castle "Beckov Castle") in [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia")

Louis did not leave a son as heir after his death in 1382. Instead, he named as his heir the young prince [Sigismund of Luxemburg](/wiki/Sigismund_of_Luxemburg "Sigismund of Luxemburg"). The Hungarian nobility did not accept his claim, and the result was an internal war. Sigismund eventually achieved total control of Hungary and established his court in Buda and Visegrád. Both palaces were rebuilt and improved, and were considered the richest of the time in Europe. Inheriting the throne of Bohemia and the Holy Roman Empire, Sigismund continued conducting his politics from Hungary, but he was kept busy fighting the [Hussites](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites") and the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), which was becoming a menace to Europe in the beginning of the 15th century.

King [Matthias Corvinus](/wiki/Matthias_Corvinus "Matthias Corvinus") of Hungary led the largest army of mercenaries of the time, the [Black Army of Hungary](/wiki/Black_Army_of_Hungary "Black Army of Hungary"), which he used to conquer [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia") and [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") and to fight the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). After Italy, Hungary was the first European country where the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") appeared.[[48]](#cite_note-49) However, the glory of the Kingdom ended in the early 16th century, when the King [Louis II of Hungary](/wiki/Louis_II_of_Hungary "Louis II of Hungary") was killed in the [Battle of Mohács](/wiki/Battle_of_Moh%C3%A1cs "Battle of Mohács") in 1526 against the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). Hungary then fell into a serious crisis and was invaded, ending its significance in central Europe during the medieval era.

### Eastern Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Eastern Europe")]

Main article: [Grand Duchy of Moscow](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Moscow "Grand Duchy of Moscow")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Europe_in_1470.PNG/250px-Europe_in_1470.PNG)](/wiki/File:Europe_in_1470.PNG)

Medieval [Russian states](/wiki/List_of_tribes_and_states_in_Belarus,_Russia_and_Ukraine "List of tribes and states in Belarus, Russia and Ukraine") c. 1470, including [Novgorod](/wiki/Novgorod_Republic "Novgorod Republic"), [Tver](/wiki/Principality_of_Tver "Principality of Tver"), [Pskov](/wiki/Pskov_Republic "Pskov Republic"), [Ryazan](/wiki/Principality_of_Ryazan "Principality of Ryazan"), [Rostov](/wiki/Rostov,_Yaroslavl_Oblast "Rostov, Yaroslavl Oblast") and [Moscow](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Moscow "Grand Duchy of Moscow")

The state of [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'") fell during the 13th century in the [Mongol invasion](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Rus "Mongol invasion of Rus").[[49]](#cite_note-50) The [Grand Duchy of Moscow](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Moscow "Grand Duchy of Moscow") rose in power thereafter, winning a great victory against the [Golden Horde](/wiki/Golden_Horde "Golden Horde") at the [Battle of Kulikovo](/wiki/Battle_of_Kulikovo "Battle of Kulikovo") in 1380.[[50]](#cite_note-51) The victory did not end Tatar rule in the region, however, and its immediate beneficiary was the [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania"), which extended its influence eastwards.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

Under the reign of [Ivan the Great](/wiki/Ivan_III_of_Russia "Ivan III of Russia") (1462–1505), Moscow became a major regional power, and the annexation of the vast [Republic of Novgorod](/wiki/Republic_of_Novgorod "Republic of Novgorod") in 1478 laid the foundations for a Russian national state.[[52]](#cite_note-53) After the [Fall of Constantinople](/wiki/Fall_of_Constantinople "Fall of Constantinople") in 1453, the Russian princes started to see themselves as the heirs of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"). They eventually took on the imperial title of [Tzar](/wiki/Tsar "Tsar"), and Moscow was described as the [Third Rome](/wiki/Third_Rome "Third Rome").[[53]](#cite_note-54)

### Southeast Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Southeast Europe")]

Main articles: [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), [Second Bulgarian Empire](/wiki/Second_Bulgarian_Empire "Second Bulgarian Empire"), [Serbian Empire](/wiki/Serbian_Empire "Serbian Empire"), [Albanian principalities](/wiki/Albanian_principalities "Albanian principalities"), and [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Siegebelgrade.jpg/250px-Siegebelgrade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siegebelgrade.jpg)

Ottoman miniature of the [siege of Belgrade](/wiki/Siege_of_Belgrade_(1456) "Siege of Belgrade (1456)") in 1456

The Byzantine Empire had for a long time dominated the [eastern Mediterranean](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean "Eastern Mediterranean") in politics and culture.[[54]](#cite_note-55) By the 14th century, however, it had almost entirely collapsed into a [tributary state of the Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Vassal_and_tributary_states_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Vassal and tributary states of the Ottoman Empire"), centered on the city of Constantinople and a few enclaves in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece").[[55]](#cite_note-56) With the [Fall of Constantinople](/wiki/Fall_of_Constantinople "Fall of Constantinople") in 1453, the Byzantine Empire was permanently extinguished.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

The [Bulgarian Empire](/wiki/Second_Bulgarian_Empire "Second Bulgarian Empire") was in decline by the 14th century, and the ascendancy of [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") was marked by the Serbian victory over the Bulgarians in the [Battle of Velbazhd](/wiki/Battle_of_Velbazhd "Battle of Velbazhd") in 1330.[[57]](#cite_note-58) By 1346, the Serbian king [Stefan Dušan](/wiki/Stefan_Du%C5%A1an "Stefan Dušan") had been proclaimed emperor.[[58]](#cite_note-59) Yet Serbian dominance was short-lived; the Serbian army led by the [Lazar Hrebeljanovic](/wiki/Lazar_Hrebeljanovic "Lazar Hrebeljanovic") was defeated by the [Ottoman Army](/wiki/Military_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Military of the Ottoman Empire") at the [Battle of Kosovo](/wiki/Battle_of_Kosovo "Battle of Kosovo") in 1389, where most of the [Serbian nobility](/wiki/Serbian_nobility "Serbian nobility") was killed and the south of the country came under [Ottoman occupation](/wiki/Ottoman_Serbia "Ottoman Serbia"), as much of [southern Bulgaria](/wiki/Southern_Bulgaria "Southern Bulgaria") had become Ottoman territory in the [Battle of Maritsa](/wiki/Battle_of_Maritsa "Battle of Maritsa") 1371.[[59]](#cite_note-Hollister,_p_p._339-60) Northern remnants of Bulgaria were finally conquered by 1396, Serbia fell in 1459, Bosnia in 1463, and Albania was finally subordinated in 1479 only a few years after the death of [Skanderbeg](/wiki/Skanderbeg "Skanderbeg"). [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade"), a Hungarian domain at the time, was the last large Balkan city to fall under Ottoman rule, in the [siege of Belgrade](/wiki/Siege_of_Belgrade_(1521) "Siege of Belgrade (1521)") of 1521. By the end of the medieval period, the entire [Balkan](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") peninsula was annexed by, or became [vassal](/wiki/Vassal "Vassal") to, the Ottomans.[[59]](#cite_note-Hollister,_p_p._339-60)

### Southwest Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Southwest Europe")]

Main articles: [Italy in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Italy_in_the_Middle_Ages "Italy in the Middle Ages"), [Spain in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Spain_in_the_Middle_Ages "Spain in the Middle Ages"), and [Portugal in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Portugal_in_the_Middle_Ages "Portugal in the Middle Ages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Batalha_de_Aljubarrota_02.jpg/250px-Batalha_de_Aljubarrota_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Batalha_de_Aljubarrota_02.jpg)

[Battle of Aljubarrota](/wiki/Battle_of_Aljubarrota "Battle of Aljubarrota") between Portugal and Castile, 1385

[Avignon](/wiki/Avignon "Avignon") was the seat of the [papacy](/wiki/Pope "Pope") from 1309 to 1376.[[60]](#cite_note-61) With the return of the Pope to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") in 1378, the [Papal State](/wiki/Papal_State "Papal State") developed into a major secular power, culminating in the morally corrupt papacy of [Alexander VI](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_VI "Pope Alexander VI").[[61]](#cite_note-62) [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") grew to prominence amongst the Italian city-states through financial business, and the dominant [Medici](/wiki/Medici "Medici") family became important promoters of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") through their patronage of the arts.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Other city-states in [northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy") also expanded their territories and consolidated their power, primarily [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), and [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa").[[63]](#cite_note-64) The [War of the Sicilian Vespers](/wiki/War_of_the_Sicilian_Vespers "War of the Sicilian Vespers") had by the early 14th century divided [southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy") into an [Aragon](/wiki/House_of_Trast%C3%A1mara "House of Trastámara") [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily") and an [Anjou](/wiki/House_of_Valois-Anjou "House of Valois-Anjou") [Kingdom of Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples").[[64]](#cite_note-65) In 1442, the two kingdoms were effectively united under Aragonese control.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

The 1469 marriage of [Isabella I of Castile](/wiki/Isabella_I_of_Castile "Isabella I of Castile") and [Ferdinand II of Aragon](/wiki/Ferdinand_II_of_Aragon "Ferdinand II of Aragon") and the 1479 death of [John II of Aragon](/wiki/John_II_of_Aragon "John II of Aragon") led to the creation of modern-day [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain").[[66]](#cite_note-67) In 1492, [Granada](/wiki/Granada "Granada") was captured from the [Moors](/wiki/Moors "Moors"), thereby completing the [Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista").[[67]](#cite_note-68) [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") had during the 15th century – particularly under [Henry the Navigator](/wiki/Henry_the_Navigator "Henry the Navigator") – gradually explored the coast of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), and in 1498, [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama "Vasco da Gama") found the sea route to [India](/wiki/India "India").[[68]](#cite_note-69) The Spanish monarchs met the Portuguese challenge by financing the expedition of [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") to find a western sea route to India, leading to the discovery of the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") in 1492.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

## Late medieval European society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Late medieval European society")]

See also: [Crisis of the Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Crisis_of_the_Late_Middle_Ages "Crisis of the Late Middle Ages"), [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"), and [Little Ice Age](/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Les_Tr%C3%A8s_Riches_Heures_du_duc_de_Berry_octobre_detail.jpg/250px-Les_Tr%C3%A8s_Riches_Heures_du_duc_de_Berry_octobre_detail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Les_Tr%C3%A8s_Riches_Heures_du_duc_de_Berry_octobre_detail.jpg)

Peasants preparing the fields for the winter with a [harrow](/wiki/Harrow_(tool) "Harrow (tool)") and sowing for the winter grain. The background shows the [Louvre castle](/wiki/Louvre_castle "Louvre castle") in Paris, c. 1410; October as depicted in the [Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry](/wiki/Tr%C3%A8s_Riches_Heures_du_Duc_de_Berry "Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry").

Around 1300–1350, the [Medieval Warm Period](/wiki/Medieval_Warm_Period "Medieval Warm Period") gave way to the [Little Ice Age](/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age").[[70]](#cite_note-71) The colder climate resulted in agricultural crises, the first of which is known as the [Great Famine of 1315–1317](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_1315%E2%80%931317 "Great Famine of 1315–1317").[[71]](#cite_note-72) The demographic consequences of this [famine](/wiki/Famine "Famine"), however, were not as severe as the [plagues](/wiki/Pandemic "Pandemic") that occurred later in the century, particularly the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death").[[72]](#cite_note-73) Estimates of the death rate caused by this epidemic range from one third to as much as sixty percent.[[73]](#cite_note-74) By around 1420, the accumulated effect of recurring plagues and famines had reduced the [population of Europe](/wiki/Population_of_Europe "Population of Europe") to perhaps no more than a third of what it was a century earlier.[[74]](#cite_note-75) The effects of natural disasters were exacerbated by armed conflicts; this was particularly the case in France during the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War").[[75]](#cite_note-76) It took 150 years for the European population to regain similar levels to 1300.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

As the European population was severely reduced, land became more plentiful for the survivors, and labour was consequently more expensive.[[77]](#cite_note-78) Attempts by landowners to forcibly reduce wages, such as the English 1351 [Statute of Laborers](/wiki/Statute_of_Laborers "Statute of Laborers"), were doomed to fail.[[78]](#cite_note-79) These efforts resulted in nothing more than fostering resentment among the peasantry, leading to rebellions such as the French [Jacquerie](/wiki/Jacquerie "Jacquerie") in 1358 and the English [Peasants' Revolt](/wiki/English_peasants%27_revolt_of_1381 "English peasants' revolt of 1381") in 1381.[[79]](#cite_note-80) The long-term effect was the virtual end of [serfdom](/wiki/Serfdom "Serfdom") in Western Europe.[[80]](#cite_note-81) In Eastern Europe, on the other hand, landowners were able to exploit the situation to force the peasantry into even more repressive bondage.[[81]](#cite_note-82)

The upheavals caused by the Black Death left certain minority groups particularly vulnerable, especially the [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew"),[[82]](#cite_note-83) who were often blamed for the calamities. [Anti-Jewish](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism") [pogroms](/wiki/Pogrom "Pogrom") were carried out all over Europe; in February 1349, two thousand Jews were murdered in [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg").[[83]](#cite_note-84) States were also guilty of discrimination against the Jews. Monarchs gave in to the demands of the people, and the Jews were expelled from England in 1290, from France in 1306, from Spain in 1492, and from Portugal in 1497.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

While the Jews were suffering persecution, one group that probably experienced increased empowerment in the late Middle Ages was women. The great social changes of the period opened up new possibilities for women in the fields of commerce, learning, and religion.[[85]](#cite_note-Klapisch-Zuber268-86) Yet at the same time, women were also vulnerable to incrimination and persecution, as belief in [witchcraft](/wiki/Witchcraft "Witchcraft") increased.[[85]](#cite_note-Klapisch-Zuber268-86)

The accumulation of social, environmental, and health-related problems also led to an increase in interpersonal violence in most parts of Europe. Population increase, religious intolerance, famine, and disease led to an increase in violent acts in vast parts of medieval society. One exception to this was North-Eastern Europe, whose population managed to maintain low levels of violence due to a more organized society resulting from extensive and successful trade.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

Up until the mid-14th century, Europe had experienced steadily increasing [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization").[[87]](#cite_note-88) Cities were also decimated by the Black Death, but the role of urban areas as centres of learning, commerce, and government ensured continued growth.[[88]](#cite_note-89) By 1500, Venice, Milan, Naples, Paris, and [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") each probably had more than 100,000 inhabitants.[[89]](#cite_note-Allmand125-90) Twenty-two other cities were larger than 40,000; most of these were in Italy and the Iberian peninsula, but there were also some in France, the Empire, and the Low Countries, as well as London in England.[[89]](#cite_note-Allmand125-90)

## Military history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Military history")]

Main article: [Medieval warfare](/wiki/Medieval_warfare "Medieval warfare")

| [Medieval warfare](/wiki/Medieval_warfare "Medieval warfare") |
| --- |
| Miniature of the [Battle of Crécy](/wiki/Battle_of_Cr%C3%A9cy "Battle of Crécy") (1346)  Manuscript of [Jean Froissart](/wiki/Jean_Froissart "Jean Froissart")'s *[Chronicles](/wiki/Froissart%27s_Chronicles "Froissart's Chronicles")*.    ---   The [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War") saw many military innovations. |

Through battles such as [Courtrai](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Golden_Spurs "Battle of the Golden Spurs") (1302), [Bannockburn](/wiki/Battle_of_Bannockburn "Battle of Bannockburn") (1314), and [Morgarten](/wiki/Battle_of_Morgarten "Battle of Morgarten") (1315), it became clear to the great territorial princes of Europe that the military advantage of the feudal [cavalry](/wiki/Cavalry "Cavalry") was lost and that a well equipped [infantry](/wiki/Infantry_in_the_Middle_Ages "Infantry in the Middle Ages") was preferable.[[90]](#cite_note-91) Through the [Welsh Wars](/wiki/Wales_in_the_Late_Middle_Ages "Wales in the Late Middle Ages"), the English became acquainted with, and adopted, the highly efficient [longbow](/wiki/English_longbow "English longbow").[[91]](#cite_note-92) Once properly managed, this weapon gave them a great advantage over the French in the Hundred Years' War.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

The introduction of [gunpowder](/wiki/Black_powder "Black powder") affected the conduct of war significantly.[[93]](#cite_note-94) Though employed by the English as early as the [Battle of Crécy](/wiki/Battle_of_Cr%C3%A9cy "Battle of Crécy") in 1346, [firearms](/wiki/Firearms "Firearms") initially had little effect in the field of battle.[[94]](#cite_note-95) It was through the use of [cannons](/wiki/Cannon "Cannon") as [siege weapons](/wiki/Siege_engine "Siege engine") that major change was brought about; the new methods would eventually change the architectural structure of [fortifications](/wiki/Fortification "Fortification").[[95]](#cite_note-96)

Changes also took place within the recruitment and composition of armies. The use of the [national](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription") or [feudal levy](/wiki/Feudal_levy "Feudal levy") was gradually replaced by paid troops of domestic [retinues](/wiki/Retinue "Retinue") or foreign [mercenaries](/wiki/Mercenary "Mercenary").[[96]](#cite_note-97) The practice was associated with [Edward III of England](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England") and the [condottieri](/wiki/Condottieri "Condottieri") of the Italian city-states.[[97]](#cite_note-98) All over Europe, [Swiss mercenaries](/wiki/Swiss_mercenaries "Swiss mercenaries") were in particularly high demand.[[98]](#cite_note-99) At the same time, the period also saw the emergence of the first permanent armies. It was in [Valois](/wiki/House_of_Valois "House of Valois") France, under the heavy demands of the Hundred Years' War, that the armed forces gradually assumed a permanent nature.[[99]](#cite_note-100)

Parallel to the military developments emerged also a constantly more elaborate [chivalric](/wiki/Chivalry "Chivalry") code of conduct for the warrior class.[[100]](#cite_note-101) This newfound ethos can be seen as a response to the diminishing military role of the aristocracy, and it gradually became almost entirely detached from its military origin.[[101]](#cite_note-102) The spirit of chivalry was given expression through the new ([secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular"))[[102]](#cite_note-103) type of [chivalric orders](/wiki/Chivalric_order "Chivalric order"); the first of these was the [Order of St. George](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_George_(Kingdom_of_Hungary) "Order of Saint George (Kingdom of Hungary)"), founded by [Charles I of Hungary](/wiki/Charles_I_of_Hungary "Charles I of Hungary") in 1325, while the best known was probably the English [Order of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter"), founded by Edward III in 1348.[[103]](#cite_note-104)

## Christian conflict and reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Christian conflict and reform")]

### The Papal Schism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: The Papal Schism")]

Main article: [Western Schism](/wiki/Western_Schism "Western Schism")

The French crown's increasing dominance over the [Papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy") culminated in the transference of the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") to [Avignon](/wiki/Avignon "Avignon") in 1309.[[104]](#cite_note-105) When the Pope returned to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") in 1377, this led to the election of different popes in Avignon and Rome, resulting in the [Western Schism](/wiki/Western_Schism "Western Schism") (1378–1417).[[105]](#cite_note-106) The Schism divided Europe along political lines; while France, her ally Scotland, and the Spanish kingdoms supported the Avignon Papacy, France's enemy England stood behind the pope in Rome, together with Portugal, Scandinavia, and most of the German princes.[[106]](#cite_note-107)

At the [Council of Constance](/wiki/Council_of_Constance "Council of Constance") (1414–1418), the Papacy was once more united in Rome.[[107]](#cite_note-108) Even though the unity of the Western Church was to last for another hundred years, and though the Papacy was to experience greater material prosperity than ever before, the Great Schism had done irreparable damage.[[108]](#cite_note-109) The internal struggles within the Church had impaired her claim to universal rule and promoted [anti-clericalism](/wiki/Anti-clericalism "Anti-clericalism") among the people and their rulers, paving the way for reform movements.[[109]](#cite_note-110)

### Protestant Reformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Protestant Reformation")]

Main articles: [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), [Hussites](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites"), and [Lollardy](/wiki/Lollardy "Lollardy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Jan_Hus_at_the_Stake.jpg/250px-Jan_Hus_at_the_Stake.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jan_Hus_at_the_Stake.jpg)

[Jan Hus](/wiki/Jan_Hus "Jan Hus") burnt at the stake

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Lutherstadt_Wittenberg_09-2016_photo06.jpg/330px-Lutherstadt_Wittenberg_09-2016_photo06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lutherstadt_Wittenberg_09-2016_photo06.jpg)

[All Saints' Church](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Church,_Wittenberg "All Saints' Church, Wittenberg") in [Wittenberg](/wiki/Wittenberg "Wittenberg"), where [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") posted his *[Ninety-five Theses](/wiki/Ninety-five_Theses "Ninety-five Theses")*, giving rise to [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism")

Though many of the events were outside the traditional time period of the Middle Ages, the end of the unity of the Western Church (the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation")) was one of the distinguishing characteristics of the medieval period.[[17]](#cite_note-Bradyxvii-17) The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") had long fought against heretic movements, but during the late Middle Ages, it started to experience demands for reform from within.[[110]](#cite_note-111) The first of these came from [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") professor [John Wycliffe](/wiki/John_Wycliffe "John Wycliffe") in England.[[111]](#cite_note-112) Wycliffe held that the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") should be the only authority in religious questions, and he spoke out against [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation"), [celibacy](/wiki/Celibacy "Celibacy"), and [indulgences](/wiki/Indulgence "Indulgence").[[112]](#cite_note-113) In spite of influential supporters among the [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") aristocracy, such as [John of Gaunt](/wiki/John_of_Gaunt "John of Gaunt"), the movement was not allowed to survive. Though Wycliffe himself was left unmolested, his supporters, the [Lollards](/wiki/Lollards "Lollards"), were eventually suppressed in England.[[113]](#cite_note-114)

The marriage of [Richard II of England](/wiki/Richard_II_of_England "Richard II of England") to [Anne of Bohemia](/wiki/Anne_of_Bohemia "Anne of Bohemia") established contacts between the two nations and brought Lollard ideas to her homeland.[[114]](#cite_note-115) The teachings of the [Czech](/wiki/Czech_lands "Czech lands") priest [Jan Hus](/wiki/Jan_Hus "Jan Hus") were based on those of John Wycliffe, yet his followers, the [Hussites](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites"), were to have a much greater political impact than the Lollards.[[115]](#cite_note-116) Hus gained a great following in [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia"), and in 1414, he was requested to appear at the Council of Constance to defend his cause.[[116]](#cite_note-117) When he was burned as a heretic in 1415, it caused a popular uprising in the Czech lands.[[117]](#cite_note-118) The subsequent [Hussite Wars](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars") fell apart due to internal quarrels and did not result in religious or national independence for the [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs"), but both the Catholic Church and the German element within the country were weakened.[[118]](#cite_note-119)

[Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther"), a German monk, started the [German Reformation](/wiki/German_Reformation "German Reformation") by posting [95 theses](/wiki/95_theses "95 theses") on the castle church of [Wittenberg](/wiki/Wittenberg "Wittenberg") on October 31, 1517.[[119]](#cite_note-120) The immediate provocation spurring this act was [Pope Leo X](/wiki/Pope_Leo_X "Pope Leo X")'s renewal of the indulgence for the building of the new [St. Peter's Basilica](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Basilica "St. Peter's Basilica") in 1514.[[120]](#cite_note-121) Luther was challenged to recant his heresy at the [Diet of Worms](/wiki/Diet_of_Worms "Diet of Worms") in 1521.[[121]](#cite_note-122) When he refused, he was placed under the ban of the Empire by [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor").[[122]](#cite_note-123) Receiving the protection of [Frederick the Wise](/wiki/Frederick_III,_Elector_of_Saxony "Frederick III, Elector of Saxony"), he was then able to translate the Bible into [German](/wiki/German_language "German language").[[123]](#cite_note-124)

To many secular rulers, the Protestant Reformation was a welcome opportunity to expand their wealth and influence.[[124]](#cite_note-125) The Catholic Church met the challenges of the reforming movements with what has been called the Catholic Reformation, or [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation").[[125]](#cite_note-126) Europe became split into northern [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") and southern Catholic parts, resulting in the Religious Wars of the 16th and 17th centuries.[[126]](#cite_note-127)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Trade and commerce")]

The increasingly dominant position of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") in the eastern [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") presented an impediment to trade for the Christian nations of the west, who in turn started looking for alternatives.[[127]](#cite_note-128) Portuguese and Spanish explorers found new trade routes – south of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") to [India](/wiki/India "India"), as well as across the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to [America](/wiki/Americas "Americas").[[128]](#cite_note-129) As [Genoese](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa") and [Venetian](/wiki/Venice "Venice") merchants opened up direct sea routes with [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), the [Champagne fairs](/wiki/Champagne_fairs "Champagne fairs") lost much of their importance.[[129]](#cite_note-130)

### Shifts in exports and the commercial revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Shifts in exports and the commercial revolution")]

At the same time, English wool export shifted from raw wool to processed cloth, resulting in losses for the cloth manufacturers of the Low Countries.[[130]](#cite_note-131) In the [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea") and [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") reached the peak of their power in the 14th century but started going into decline in the fifteenth.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

In the late 13th and early 14th centuries, a process took place – primarily in Italy but partly also in the Empire – that historians have termed a "commercial revolution".[[132]](#cite_note-133) Among the innovations of the period were new forms of [partnership](/wiki/Corporation "Corporation") and the issuing of [insurance](/wiki/Insurance "Insurance"), both of which contributed to reducing the risk of commercial ventures; the [bill of exchange](/wiki/Negotiable_instrument#Bill_of_exchange "Negotiable instrument") and other forms of credit that circumvented the [canonical laws](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law") for [gentiles](/wiki/Gentile "Gentile") against [usury](/wiki/Usury "Usury") and eliminated the dangers of carrying [bullion](/wiki/Precious_metal "Precious metal"); and new forms of [accounting](/wiki/Accounting "Accounting"), in particular [double-entry bookkeeping](/wiki/Double-entry_bookkeeping_system "Double-entry bookkeeping system"), which allowed for better oversight and accuracy.[[133]](#cite_note-134)

With the financial expansion, trading rights became more jealously guarded by the commercial elite. Towns saw the growing power of [guilds](/wiki/Guilds_in_the_Middle_Ages "Guilds in the Middle Ages"), while on a national level, special companies would be granted monopolies on particular trades, like [the English wool Staple](/wiki/The_English_wool_Staple "The English wool Staple").[[134]](#cite_note-135) The beneficiaries of these developments would accumulate immense wealth. Families like the [Fuggers](/wiki/Fugger_family "Fugger family") in Germany, the [Medicis](/wiki/House_of_Medici "House of Medici") in Italy, and the [de la Poles](/wiki/Duke_of_Suffolk "Duke of Suffolk") in England and individuals like [Jacques Cœur](/wiki/Jacques_C%C5%93ur "Jacques Cœur") in France would help finance the wars of kings, achieving great political influence in the process.[[135]](#cite_note-136)

Though there is no doubt that the demographic crisis of the 14th century caused a dramatic fall in production and commerce in *absolute* terms, there has been a vigorous historical debate over whether the decline was greater than the fall in population.[[136]](#cite_note-137) While the older orthodoxy held that the artistic output of the Renaissance was a result of greater opulence, more recent studies have suggested that there might have been a so-called "depression of the Renaissance".[[137]](#cite_note-138) In spite of convincing arguments for the case, the statistical evidence is simply too incomplete for a definite conclusion to be made.[[138]](#cite_note-139)

## Arts and sciences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Arts and sciences")]

In the 14th century, the predominant academic trend of [scholasticism](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism") was challenged by the [humanist](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism") movement. Though primarily an attempt to revitalise the [classical languages](/wiki/Classical_language "Classical language"), the movement also led to innovations within the fields of science, art, and literature, helped by impulses from [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") scholars who had to seek refuge in the West after the [Fall of Constantinople](/wiki/Fall_of_Constantinople "Fall of Constantinople") in 1453.[[139]](#cite_note-140)

In science, classical authorities like [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") were challenged for the first time since antiquity. Within the arts, humanism took the form of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"). Though the 15th-century Renaissance was a highly localised phenomenon – limited mostly to the city-states of northern Italy – artistic developments were taking place also further north, particularly in the Netherlands.[[a]](#cite_note-For_references,_see_below-18)

### Philosophy, science and technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Philosophy, science and technology")]

Main articles: [Medieval philosophy](/wiki/Medieval_philosophy "Medieval philosophy"), [History of science in the Middle Ages](/wiki/History_of_science_in_the_Middle_Ages "History of science in the Middle Ages"), and [Medieval technology](/wiki/Medieval_technology "Medieval technology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/European_Output_of_Manuscripts_500%E2%80%931500.png/250px-European_Output_of_Manuscripts_500%E2%80%931500.png)](/wiki/File:European_Output_of_Manuscripts_500%E2%80%931500.png)

European output of manuscripts (500–1500). The rising trend in medieval book production saw its continuation in the period.[[140]](#cite_note-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Printing_towns_incunabula.svg/330px-Printing_towns_incunabula.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Printing_towns_incunabula.svg)

[Spread of printing](/wiki/Global_spread_of_the_printing_press "Global spread of the printing press") by [Johannes Gutenberg](/wiki/Johannes_Gutenberg "Johannes Gutenberg") from [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz") in Europe in the 15th century

The predominant school of thought in the 13th century was the [Thomistic](/wiki/Thomism "Thomism") reconciliation of the teachings of [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") with [Christian theology](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology").[[141]](#cite_note-142) The [Condemnation of 1277](/wiki/Condemnations_(University_of_Paris)#Condemnation_of_1277 "Condemnations (University of Paris)"), enacted at the [University of Paris](/wiki/University_of_Paris "University of Paris"), placed restrictions on ideas that could be interpreted as heretical, restrictions that had implication for [Aristotelian](/wiki/Aristotelianism "Aristotelianism") thought.[[142]](#cite_note-SEP_1277-143) An alternative was presented by [William of Ockham](/wiki/William_of_Ockham "William of Ockham"), following the manner of the earlier Franciscan [John Duns Scotus](/wiki/John_Duns_Scotus "John Duns Scotus"), who insisted that the world of reason and the world of faith had to be kept apart. Ockham introduced the principle of parsimony – or [Occam's razor](/wiki/Occam%27s_razor "Occam's razor") – whereby a simple theory is preferred to a more complex one and speculation on unobservable phenomena is avoided.[[143]](#cite_note-144) This maxim is, however, often misquoted. Occam was referring to his nominalism in this quotation. Essentially saying the theory of absolutes, or metaphysical realism, was unnecessary to make sense of the world.

This new approach liberated scientific speculation from the dogmatic restraints of Aristotelian science and paved the way for new approaches. Particularly within the field of theories of [motion](/wiki/Motion_(physics) "Motion (physics)"), great advances were made, when such scholars as [Jean Buridan](/wiki/Jean_Buridan "Jean Buridan"), [Nicole Oresme](/wiki/Nicole_Oresme "Nicole Oresme"), and the [Oxford Calculators](/wiki/Oxford_Calculators "Oxford Calculators") challenged the work of Aristotle.[[144]](#cite_note-145) Buridan developed the theory of *impetus* as the cause of the motion of projectiles, which was an important step towards the modern concept of [inertia](/wiki/Inertia "Inertia").[[145]](#cite_note-146) The works of these scholars anticipated the [heliocentric](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism") worldview of [Nicolaus Copernicus](/wiki/Nicolaus_Copernicus "Nicolaus Copernicus").[[146]](#cite_note-147)

Certain technological inventions of the period – whether of [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") or [Chinese](/wiki/China "China") origin or unique European innovations – were to have great influence on political and social developments, in particular [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder"), the [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press"), and the [compass](/wiki/Compass "Compass"). The introduction of gunpowder to the field of battle not only affected military organisation but also helped advance the nation-state. [Gutenberg](/wiki/Johann_Gutenberg "Johann Gutenberg")'s [movable type](/wiki/Movable_type "Movable type") [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press") made possible not only the [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") but also a dissemination of knowledge that would lead to a gradually more egalitarian society. The [compass](/wiki/Compass "Compass"), along with other innovations such as the [cross-staff](/wiki/Cross-staff "Cross-staff"), the [mariner's astrolabe](/wiki/Mariner%27s_astrolabe "Mariner's astrolabe"), and advances in shipbuilding, enabled the navigation of the [World Oceans](/wiki/World_Ocean "World Ocean") and the early phases of [colonialism](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism").[[147]](#cite_note-148) Other inventions had a greater impact on everyday life, such as [eyeglasses](/wiki/Glasses "Glasses") and the weight-driven [clock](/wiki/Clock "Clock").[[148]](#cite_note-149)

### Visual arts and architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Visual arts and architecture")]

Main articles: [Medieval art](/wiki/Medieval_art "Medieval art") and [Medieval architecture](/wiki/Medieval_architecture "Medieval architecture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/MZK_003_Nr_01_Fig_27_-_Holzwohnhaus_Halberstadt_Aufbau.jpg/250px-MZK_003_Nr_01_Fig_27_-_Holzwohnhaus_Halberstadt_Aufbau.jpg)](/wiki/File:MZK_003_Nr_01_Fig_27_-_Holzwohnhaus_Halberstadt_Aufbau.jpg)

Urban dwelling house, late 15th century, [Halberstadt](/wiki/Halberstadt "Halberstadt"), Germany

A precursor to [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") art can be seen already in the early 14th-century works of [Giotto](/wiki/Giotto_di_Bondone "Giotto di Bondone"). Giotto was the first painter since antiquity to attempt the representation of three-dimensional reality and endow his characters with true human emotions.[[149]](#cite_note-150) The most important developments, however, came in 15th-century Florence. The affluence of the merchant class allowed extensive patronage of the arts, and foremost among the patrons were the Medici.[[150]](#cite_note-151)

The period saw several important technical innovations, like the principle of [linear perspective](/wiki/Perspective_(graphical) "Perspective (graphical)") found in the work of [Masaccio](/wiki/Masaccio "Masaccio") and later described by [Brunelleschi](/wiki/Filippo_Brunelleschi "Filippo Brunelleschi").[[151]](#cite_note-152) Greater realism was also achieved through the scientific study of anatomy, championed by artists like [Donatello](/wiki/Donatello "Donatello").[[152]](#cite_note-153) This can be seen particularly well in his sculptures, inspired by the study of classical models.[[153]](#cite_note-154) As the centre of the movement shifted to Rome, the period culminated in the [High Renaissance](/wiki/High_Renaissance "High Renaissance") masters [da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo"), and [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael").[[154]](#cite_note-155)

The ideas of the [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") were slow to cross the Alps into northern Europe, but important artistic innovations were made also in the Low Countries.[[155]](#cite_note-156) Though not – as previously believed – the inventor of oil painting, [Jan van Eyck](/wiki/Jan_van_Eyck "Jan van Eyck") was a champion of the new medium and used it to create works of great realism and minute detail.[[156]](#cite_note-157) The two cultures influenced each other and learned from each other, but painting in the Netherlands remained more focused on textures and surfaces than the idealized compositions of Italy.[[157]](#cite_note-158)

In northern European countries, [Gothic architecture](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") remained the norm, and the Gothic cathedral was further elaborated.[[158]](#cite_note-159) In Italy, on the other hand, architecture took a different direction, also here inspired by classical ideals. The crowning work of the period was the [Santa Maria del Fiore](/wiki/Santa_Maria_del_Fiore "Santa Maria del Fiore") in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), with Giotto's clock tower, [Ghiberti](/wiki/Lorenzo_Ghiberti "Lorenzo Ghiberti")'s baptistery gates, and [Brunelleschi](/wiki/Filippo_Brunelleschi "Filippo Brunelleschi")'s cathedral [dome](/wiki/Dome "Dome") of unprecedented proportions.[[159]](#cite_note-160)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Literature")]

Further information: [Medieval literature](/wiki/Medieval_literature "Medieval literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Dante_Domenico_di_Michelino.jpg/250px-Dante_Domenico_di_Michelino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dante_Domenico_di_Michelino.jpg)

[Dante](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri") as portrayed by [Domenico di Michelino](/wiki/Domenico_di_Michelino "Domenico di Michelino"), from a fresco painted in 1465

The most important development of late medieval literature was the ascendancy of the [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") languages.[[160]](#cite_note-161) The vernacular had been in use in England since the 8th century and France since the 11th century, where the most popular genres had been the [chanson de geste](/wiki/Chanson_de_geste "Chanson de geste"), [troubadour lyrics](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour"), and romantic epics, or the [romance](/wiki/Romance_(heroic_literature) "Romance (heroic literature)").[[161]](#cite_note-162) Though Italy was later in evolving a native literature in the vernacular language, it was here that the most important developments of the period were to come.[[162]](#cite_note-163)

[Dante Alighieri](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri")'s *[Divine Comedy](/wiki/The_Divine_Comedy "The Divine Comedy")*, written in the early 14th century, merged a medieval worldview with classical ideals.[[163]](#cite_note-164) Another promoter of the [Italian language](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") was [Boccaccio](/wiki/Giovanni_Boccaccio "Giovanni Boccaccio") with his *[Decameron](/wiki/The_Decameron "The Decameron")*.[[164]](#cite_note-165) The application of the vernacular did not entail a rejection of [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), and both Dante and Boccaccio wrote prolifically in Latin as well as Italian, as would [Petrarch](/wiki/Petrarch "Petrarch") later (whose *[Canzoniere](/wiki/Il_Canzoniere "Il Canzoniere")* also promoted the vernacular and whose contents are considered the first modern [lyric poems](/wiki/Lyric_poem "Lyric poem")).[[165]](#cite_note-166) Together, the three poets established the [Tuscan dialect](/wiki/Tuscan_dialect "Tuscan dialect") as the norm for the modern [Italian language](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language").[[166]](#cite_note-167)

The new literary style spread rapidly and in France, influenced such writers as [Eustache Deschamps](/wiki/Eustache_Deschamps "Eustache Deschamps") and [Guillaume de Machaut](/wiki/Guillaume_de_Machaut "Guillaume de Machaut").[[167]](#cite_note-168) In England, [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer") helped establish [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") as a literary language with his *[Canterbury Tales](/wiki/The_Canterbury_Tales "The Canterbury Tales")*, which contained a wide variety of narrators and stories (including some translated from Boccaccio).[[168]](#cite_note-169) The spread of vernacular literature eventually reached as far as Bohemia and the Baltic, Slavic, and Byzantine worlds.[[169]](#cite_note-170)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Medieval music](/wiki/Medieval_music "Medieval music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Vielle.jpg/250px-Vielle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vielle.jpg)

A musician plays the [vielle](/wiki/Vielle "Vielle") in a 14th-century [Medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") [manuscript](/wiki/Manuscript "Manuscript").

Music was an important part of both secular and spiritual culture, and in the universities, it made up part of the *[quadrivium](/wiki/Quadrivium "Quadrivium")* of the liberal arts.[[170]](#cite_note-171) From the early 13th century, the dominant sacred musical form had been the [motet](/wiki/Motet "Motet"), a composition with text in several parts.[[171]](#cite_note-172) From the 1330s and onwards emerged the [polyphonic](/wiki/Polyphony "Polyphony") style, which was a more complex fusion of independent voices.[[172]](#cite_note-173) Polyphony had been common in the secular music of the [Provençal](/wiki/Provence "Provence") [troubadours](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour"). Many of these had fallen victim to the 13th-century [Albigensian Crusade](/wiki/Albigensian_Crusade "Albigensian Crusade"), but their influence reached the papal court at Avignon.[[173]](#cite_note-174)

The main representatives of the new style, often referred to as *[ars nova](/wiki/Ars_nova "Ars nova")* as opposed to *[ars antiqua](/wiki/Ars_antiqua "Ars antiqua")*, were the composers [Philippe de Vitry](/wiki/Philippe_de_Vitry "Philippe de Vitry") and [Guillaume de Machaut](/wiki/Guillaume_de_Machaut "Guillaume de Machaut").[[174]](#cite_note-175) In Italy, where the Provençal troubadours had also found refuge, the corresponding period goes under the name of [trecento](/wiki/Music_of_the_Trecento "Music of the Trecento"), and the leading composers were [Giovanni da Cascia](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Cascia "Giovanni da Cascia"), [Jacopo da Bologna](/wiki/Jacopo_da_Bologna "Jacopo da Bologna"), and [Francesco Landini](/wiki/Francesco_Landini "Francesco Landini").[[175]](#cite_note-176) A prominent reformer of [Orthodox Church music](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_music "Eastern Orthodox music") from the first half of the 14th century was [John Kukuzelis](/wiki/John_Kukuzelis "John Kukuzelis"); he also introduced a system of notation widely used in the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") in the following centuries.

### Theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Theatre")]

Main article: [Medieval theatre](/wiki/Medieval_theatre "Medieval theatre")

In the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"), plays were produced in some 127 different towns during the Middle Ages. These vernacular [Mystery plays](/wiki/Mystery_plays "Mystery plays") were written in cycles of a large number of plays: [York](/wiki/York_Mystery_Plays "York Mystery Plays") (48 plays), [Chester](/wiki/Chester_Mystery_Plays "Chester Mystery Plays") (24), [Wakefield](/wiki/Wakefield_Mystery_Plays "Wakefield Mystery Plays") (32), and [Unknown](/wiki/N-Town_Plays "N-Town Plays") (42). A larger number of plays survive from [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") in this period, and some type of religious drama was performed in nearly every European country in the late Middle Ages. Many of these plays contained [comedy](/wiki/Comedy "Comedy"), [devils](/wiki/Devil "Devil"), [villains](/wiki/Villain "Villain"), and [clowns](/wiki/Clown "Clown").[[176]](#cite_note-177)

[Morality plays](/wiki/Morality_plays "Morality plays") emerged as a distinct dramatic form around 1400 and flourished until 1550, an example being *[The Castle of Perseverance](/wiki/The_Castle_of_Perseverance "The Castle of Perseverance")*, which depicts [mankind](/wiki/Human "Human")'s progress from birth to death. Another famous morality play is *[Everyman](/wiki/Everyman_(15th-century_play) "Everyman (15th-century play)")*. Everyman receives [Death](/wiki/Death "Death")'s summons, struggles to escape, and finally resigns himself to necessity. Along the way, he is deserted by [Kindred](/wiki/Kinship "Kinship"), [Goods](/wiki/Good_(economics) "Good (economics)"), and Fellowship – only [Good Deeds](/wiki/Good_works "Good works") goes with him to the grave.

At the end of the late Middle Ages, professional actors began to appear in [England](/wiki/England "England") and [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). [Richard III](/wiki/Richard_III_of_England "Richard III of England") and [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") both maintained small companies of professional actors. Their plays were performed in the [Great Hall](/wiki/Great_Hall "Great Hall") of a nobleman's residence, often with a raised platform at one end for the audience and a "screen" at the other for the actors. Also important were [Mummers' plays](/wiki/Mummers_Play "Mummers Play"), performed during the [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") season, and court [masques](/wiki/Masque "Masque"). These masques were especially popular during the reign of [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") who had a House of Revels built and an [Office of Revels](/wiki/Master_of_the_Revels "Master of the Revels") established in 1545.[[177]](#cite_note-BH101-178)

The end of medieval drama came about due to a number of factors, including the weakening power of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), and the banning of religious plays in many countries. [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") forbid all religious plays in 1558, and the great cycle plays had been silenced by the 1580s. Similarly, religious plays were banned in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") in 1539, the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States") in 1547, and [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") in 1548. The abandonment of these plays destroyed the international theatre that had thereto existed and forced each country to develop its own form of drama. It also allowed dramatists to turn to secular subjects and the reviving interest in [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") theatre provided them with the perfect opportunity.[[177]](#cite_note-BH101-178)

### After the Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: After the Middle Ages")]

Main article: [Early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period")

After the end of the late Middle Ages period, the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") spread unevenly over continental Europe from the southern European region. The intellectual transformation of the Renaissance is viewed as a bridge between the Middle Ages and the Modern era. Europeans would later begin an [era of world discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"). Combined with the influx of classical ideas was the invention of [printing](/wiki/Printing "Printing") which facilitated dissemination of the printed word and democratized learning. These two things would lead to the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"). Europeans also discovered new trading routes, as was the case with [Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus")' travel to the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") in 1492, and [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama "Vasco da Gama")'s circumnavigation of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") and [India](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") in 1498. Their discoveries strengthened the economy and power of European nations.

## Ottomans and Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Late_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Ottomans and Europe")]

Main article: [Ottoman wars in Europe](/wiki/Ottoman_wars_in_Europe "Ottoman wars in Europe")

| Ottomans and Europe | |
| --- | --- |
| Saint [John of Capistrano](/wiki/John_of_Capistrano "John of Capistrano") and the Hungarian armies fighting the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") at the [siege of Belgrade](/wiki/Siege_of_Belgrade_(1456) "Siege of Belgrade (1456)") in 1456 |
| King [Matthias Corvinus](/wiki/Matthias_Corvinus "Matthias Corvinus")'s Black Army campaigns |

By the end of the 15th century, the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") had advanced all over [Southeast Europe](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe"), eventually conquering the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") and extending control over the Balkan states. Hungary was the last bastion of the Latin Christian world in the East, and fought to keep its rule over a period of two centuries. After the death of the young king [Vladislaus I of Hungary](/wiki/Vladislaus_I_of_Hungary "Vladislaus I of Hungary") during the [Battle of Varna](/wiki/Battle_of_Varna "Battle of Varna") in 1444 against the Ottomans, the Kingdom was placed in the hands of Count [John Hunyadi](/wiki/John_Hunyadi "John Hunyadi"), who became Hungary's regent-governor (1446–1453). Hunyadi was considered one of the most relevant military figures of the 15th century: Pope [Pius II](/wiki/Pius_II "Pius II") awarded him the title of *[Athleta Christi](/wiki/Athleta_Christi "Athleta Christi")*, or Champion of Christ, for being the only hope of resisting the Ottomans from advancing to Central and Western Europe.

Hunyadi succeeded during the [siege of Belgrade](/wiki/Siege_of_Belgrade_(1456) "Siege of Belgrade (1456)") in 1456 against the Ottomans, the biggest victory against that empire in decades. This battle became a real crusade against the Muslims, as the peasants were motivated by the [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans") friar Saint [John of Capistrano](/wiki/John_of_Capistrano "John of Capistrano"), who came from Italy predicating him holy war. The effect that it created in that time was one of the main factors that helped in achieving the victory. However the premature death of the Hungarian lord left [Pannonia](/wiki/Pannonia "Pannonia") defenseless and in chaos. In an extremely unusual event for the Middle Ages, Hunyadi's son, Matthias, was elected as [king of Hungary](/wiki/King_of_Hungary "King of Hungary") by the [Hungarian nobility](/wiki/Hungarian_nobility "Hungarian nobility"). For the first time, a member of an aristocratic family (and not from a royal family) was crowned.

King [Matthias Corvinus](/wiki/Matthias_Corvinus "Matthias Corvinus") of [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") (1458–1490) was one of the most prominent figures of the period, directing campaigns to the West, conquering Bohemia in answer to the pope's call for help against the Hussite Protestants. Also, in resolving political hostilities with the German emperor [Frederick III of Habsburg](/wiki/Frederick_III,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Frederick III, Holy Roman Emperor"), he invaded his western domains. Matthew organized the [Black Army](/wiki/Black_Army_of_Hungary "Black Army of Hungary") of mercenary soldiers; it was considered as the biggest army of its time. Using this powerful tool, the Hungarian king led wars against the Turkish armies and stopped the Ottomans during his reign. After the death of Matthew, and with end of the Black Army, the Ottoman Empire grew in strength and Central Europe was defenseless. At the [Battle of Mohács](/wiki/Battle_of_Moh%C3%A1cs "Battle of Mohács"), the forces of the Ottoman Empire annihilated the Hungarian army and [Louis II of Hungary](/wiki/Louis_II_of_Hungary "Louis II of Hungary") drowned in the Csele Creek while trying to escape. The leader of the Hungarian army, Pál Tomori, also died in the battle. This is considered to be one of the final battles of medieval times.

* 1337: The [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War") begins

* 1347: The [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") begins
* 1347: [University of Prague](/wiki/Charles_University_in_Prague "Charles University in Prague") was founded
* 1348: [Giovanni Villani](/wiki/Giovanni_Villani "Giovanni Villani") finishes work on *[Nuova Cronica](/wiki/Nuova_Cronica "Nuova Cronica")*
* 1348–1349: [Byzantine–Genoese War](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Genoese_War_(1348%E2%80%9349) "Byzantine–Genoese War (1348–49)")
* 1362: [Battle of Blue Waters](/wiki/Battle_of_Blue_Waters "Battle of Blue Waters")

Lithuania defeats Golden Horde. Principality of Kiev becomes part of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania

* 1364: [Jagiellonian University](/wiki/Jagiellonian_University "Jagiellonian University") was founded

* 1376: [Avignon Papacy](/wiki/Avignon_Papacy "Avignon Papacy") ended
* 1380: [Battle of Kulikovo](/wiki/Battle_of_Kulikovo "Battle of Kulikovo")
* 1380: The *[Canterbury Tales](/wiki/Canterbury_Tales "Canterbury Tales")*
* 1381: [Peasants' Revolt](/wiki/Peasants%27_Revolt "Peasants' Revolt") (England)
* 1381: [John Wycliffe](/wiki/John_Wycliffe "John Wycliffe") translated the Bible
* 1385: [Union of Krewo](/wiki/Union_of_Krewo "Union of Krewo"), initiation of the [Polish–Lithuanian union](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_union "Polish–Lithuanian union")
* 1385: [Battle of Aljubarrota](/wiki/Battle_of_Aljubarrota "Battle of Aljubarrota")
* 1386: [University of Heidelberg](/wiki/University_of_Heidelberg "University of Heidelberg") was founded
* 1389: [Battle of Kosovo](/wiki/Battle_of_Kosovo "Battle of Kosovo")—Serbian and Bosnian forces defeated by the Ottomans
* 1342–1392: Partitioning of the [Kingdom of Rus](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Rus "Kingdom of Rus") (Galicia) between Poland and Lithuania ([Galicia–Volhynia Wars](/wiki/Galicia%E2%80%93Volhynia_Wars "Galicia–Volhynia Wars"))
* 1396: [Battle of Nicopolis](/wiki/Battle_of_Nicopolis "Battle of Nicopolis") and first Ottoman conquest in Europe
